danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy creature spawns and supernatural entities in level progression
danger_signals.meaning: The Monster's magical dominion over mythical forces (Death, Medusa) signals escalating threat

danger_signals.visual_form: Destructed environmental state, burnt structures, abandoned settlements
danger_signals.meaning: World-state indicator of Monster's conquering advance and civilian danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Captive character narrative state, likely shown in cutscenes or final level design
danger_signals.meaning: Highest-stakes objective; rescue mission frames the danger as personal/emotional

danger_signals.visual_form: Boss creatures with supernatural appearance (Death, Medusa visual forms)
danger_signals.meaning: Magic as tangible threat multiplier; enslaved mythical forces indicate dark sorcery

safety_signals.visual_form: Clubs and swords as collectible items appearing in levels
safety_signals.meaning: Progression toward player agency; player transitions from bare-handed to armed readiness

safety_signals.visual_form: Fireball magic ability as discoverable pickup
safety_signals.meaning: Offensive capability boost; player gains supernatural counter-ability

safety_signals.visual_form: Human civilization as level origin point
safety_signals.meaning: Home base/safe zone implies familiar territory before descent into Monster's domain

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Magic/sorcery visual motifs, possibly undead/creature aesthetic
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Mythical entities (Death, Medusa) as enslaved minions; general supernatural creature types
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Raided villages, destroyed settlements, corrupted landscapes

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Young swordsman protagonist as representative; Emily as civilian objective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Villages raized and destroyed; environmental devastation visible in level design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Supernatural army actively threatens; enemies spawn and patrol conquered territories

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mythical entities (Death, Medusa) appear as corrupted/enslaved visual forms
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: These boss enemies exhibit controlled hostile behavior toward player; demonstrate magic's power

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Emily's kidnapping; potentially visible through cutscenes or environmental clues (maiden's chambers, chains)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Quest objective remains active; rescue mission drives player progression forward

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Weapons and magic pickups scattered through level progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player transitions from vulnerable (bare-handed) to combat-capable (armed/magic)

environmental_storytelling.example: Raided villages visible as burnt/ruined structures communicate Monster's conquering path and stakes
Increasing supernatural enemy types signal deeper penetration into Monster's domain and rising danger
Mythical entities (Death, Medusa) as dungeon bosses signal Monster's magical power and enslaved forces
Progressive weapon discovery (clubs, swords) marks player's journey from vulnerable to capable
Young swordsman against supernatural army establishes David-vs-Goliath storytelling through character role alone

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Commands magical army; enslaves mythical entities; kidnaps Emily as bride; actively threatens villages
Patrol and attack in levels; demonstrate hostile intent; respawn behavior indicates magic sustains them
Boss-level encounters; appear as corrupted versions; attack with supernatural abilities
Progresses through levels collecting weapons/magic; moves toward Monster's stronghold; rescue mission frame
Remains kidnapped; likely appears in final confrontation or end-state scene; motivates player progression

notes: Frankenstein: The Monster Returns uses classic Castlevania-style adventure framing—side-scrolling platformer with beat-em-up elements. World-state communication relies heavily on narrative framing (kidnapping, village destruction) combined with environmental design (destroyed settlements) and enemy visual/behavioral manifestation. Danger signals are thematic (supernatural army, enslaved mythical forces) rather than explicit mechanical tells. The game communicates faction identity through simple binaries: human civilization (player) vs. magical supernatural invasion (Monster's domain). Safety signals tied to weapon/magic acquisition provide clear progression feedback. Limited source material constrains specificity on exact visual design choices, but pattern is consistent with 1991 NES action-adventure conventions.
